calendar.matters:
Notice Of Motion And Hearing On Petition For Writ Of Ordinary Mandate
Off Calendar Jul-08-2022 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES:
Attys. MATTHEW TAYLOR, Esq. and GARY M. MESSING, ESQ. of Messing Adam & Jasmine LLP (Tel: 415-266-1800) for petitioner Cal Fire Local 2881 (via Zoom videoconference;
Atty. NATASHA SAGGAR SHETH, Esq. of California Attorney General's Office (Tel: 415-510-3818) for respondent California Department of Forestry and Fire Protection.

HEARING RE: PETITIONER CAL FIRE 2881'S Hearing On Petition For Writ Of Ordinary Mandate.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative rulings.

Cal Fire Local 2881's "petition for writ of ordinary mandate to compel release of public records" is denied.

In July 2021, the state of California mandated that many of its employees either verify Covid-19 vaccination status or regularly test for the virus. A private company, Color Health, Inc., contracted to do testing. Color collects employee information and test samples and results.

Cal Fire Local 2881 is a labor union that represents Department of Forestry and Fire Protection (Cal Fire) employees. The union met and conferred with Cal Fire over concerns about Covid-19 measures and requested documents under the public records act.

The union says the "sole request at issue in this petition, asks for the following information: 'The identities of all CAL FIRE employees who have registered for testing with Color Health, Inc. and have waived their privacy rights through this registration process.'" (Memo. 5:9-12.) Cal Fire asserts that the registration with Color has not, in fact, waived privacy rights, and the union has limited its records request to its members only. (Opp. 6:17-23; Memo. 5:24-25.) Thus, the issue is whether Cal Fire is required to disclose records identifying union employees who have registered for testing with Color - and hence, in many cases, reveal their unvaccinated status to the public.

I agree with Cal Fire that employees' medical privacy protections bar public disclosure of the records. These protections are found in the public records act as well as other statutory, case and constitutional law. (Govt. Code sec. 6254(c); John B. v. Sup. Ct. (2006) 38 Cal.4th 1177, 1198; Los Angeles Gay & Lesbian Center v. Sup. Ct. (2011) 194 Cal.App.4th 288, 308; Cal. Const., art. I, sec. 1; 29 CFR sec. 1630.14(c)(1); 45 CFR sec. 164.502.)

The union argues that it seeks "the mere disclosure of names." (Memo. 8:17.) However, with the names would come public disclosure of many employees' unvaccinated status - a sensitive medical fact today. Further, where "a requester has an alternative, less intrusive, means of obtaining the information sought, the public interest in disclosure is minimal." (Los Angeles Unified School Dist. v. Sup. Ct. (2014) 228 Cal.App.4th 222, 242.) As Cal Fire points out, the union has a less intrusive means to learn whether its members have registered for testing: ask them.

In its reply brief, the union argues that some of the employees tested by Color "may" be vaccinated, but offers no evidence of the number. (Rply. 5:19.) Moreover, the public's assumption would no doubt be that the large majority of tested employees are unvaccinated.
Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Laura Espinosa, CSR No. 11400, Email: lauraespinosacourtreporting@gmail.com, Tel: 925-436-6827 (302/RBU)
